content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Admissions and intake-equity regulation (keep structure, fix the gate)'. Early selection at age 10–12 into parallel 6- and 8-year grammar schools is the primary structural cause of Hungary's current position as Europe's most socially selective education system and should be phased out by 2035 in favor of single-entry-age (14 or 15) and unified secondary-school admission to reduce inequality of opportunity without sacrificing demonstrable value-added in pedagogy.
opportunities: Reduced SES-skew in admitted cohorts at the 6/8-year gate, addressing the documented near-zero intake share of multiply-disadvantaged pupils.
Preserves the approximately 10% competency-gain advantage associated with 6/8-year tracks for admitted students, since the track structure itself is untouched.
strengths: Approximately 8–12% of a Hungarian age cohort enters 6- or 8-year grammar school (gimnazium) tracks, selected at age 10 or 12 largely via central written examination; entrants are strongly skewed toward high-SES, urban families.

Since 2022, Hungary has become Europe's most socially selective school system according to PISA; Hungary's social selectivity index worsened by 5.3 points between 2018 and 2022, the largest deterioration in Europe alongside Poland's tracking re-introduction.
threats: Implementation by ministerial decree rather than cardinal law leaves the mechanism exposed to reversal by a subsequent government within a single electoral cycle; the scenario does not propose a specific entrenchment design (e.g., cardinal-law status, cross-party pact, or sunset-clause safeguard) to mitigate this.
Fidesz's demonstrated framing of any selection-related change as ideologically totalitarian creates reversal risk even for a narrower admissions-only intervention, not only for structural abolition.
weaknesses: What is the true causal magnitude of peer effects vs. pedagogical quality in the observed 10% value-added in elite tracks? Current research separates them only weakly.
How much of the performance gap between grammar and vocational students would narrow if school choice and catchment sorting were held constant and only early selection were removed?
